name: Dosa
recipeIngredient: 2 cups Rice (Fat Rice, if possible, or plain)
recipeInstructions:
Dal Masala (Spicy Powder):
1/2 cup Dry Dhania Seeds (Coriander)
1/4 cup Cumin Seeds
3 cloves (or 4)
1/8 cup Whole Black Peppercorns
Handful Dried Red Chilies
1/2 tsp Hing (Asafoetida)
Handful Channa Dal
Handful Urad Dal
1 stick Cinnamon (or 2)
15 leaves of Limbu (to 15)
Rassam:
1 cup Toovar Dal
1.5 pints Water
2 tbsp Dosa Garam Masala (to 3 tbsp), as above
Handful Channa Dal
Handful Urad Dal
1 tsp Rai Seeds (Mustard Seeds)
4 leaves of Limbu (to 5)
2 tbsp Oil
Salt - to taste
3/4 tsp Haldi Powder (Turmeric)
1/4 tsp Hing (Asafoetida)
2 tbsp Amli Paste (Tamarind)
Dosa Stuffing (Vegetable):
5 large potatoes (to 6) - washed and diced
2 med Onions, chopped - optional
Handful Urad Dal
4 leaves of Limbu (to 5)
1 tsp Rai Seeds (Mustard Seeds)
1 tsp Jeera Seeds (Cumin)
Salt - to taste
1 tsp Haldi Powder (Turmeric)
1 tbsp Dhania Jerru Powder (Coriander Powder)
2 green chilies - optional
1 tsp Ginger Paste
2 tbsp Oil (to 3 tbsp)
2 tbsp Lemon Juice - optional
Coconut Chutney:
1 cup Dessicated Coconut (not sweet)
Handful Urad Dal
1 tsp Rai Seeds (Mustard Seeds)
1 tbsp Oil
2 leaves of Limbu (to 3)
2 cups Plain Yogurt
1 green chili, finely chopped - optional
Green Dhania for garnish
Salt - to taste
Dosa are a staple food available throughout India, originally from the south. They can be eaten at breakfast, lunch, or dinner and are typically accompanied by coconut chutney and dal. The dal can be either spicy Rassam or thick and soupy. This recipe is provided by my sister, who is the greatest dosa maker in the world.
For Dosa Mix (Pancake):
Wash and mix all the dals, soak for at least 5 to 6 hours, then blend into a smooth pancake-like mixture. Leave to ferment overnight in a warm place such as your boiler cupboard. You can also store any extra pancake mixture in the fridge for a couple of days or freeze it for later use.
Make Dal Masala:
To make authentic tasting dal, you need to prepare the dal masala first. This requires some extra work! If you can't handle it, you can get ready-made garam masala from stores and supermarkets nowadays. However, if you have a little time, it's worth the extra effort as you can store the homemade masala in an airtight container for quite some time.
Take a heavy-based pan over very low heat, add channa dal and urad dal, roast until fragrant and slightly red. Add all other ingredients and roast gently until heated through with a spicy aroma. Leave to cool, then blend into a powder. This can be kept in an airtight container until required.
Rassam:
Thin and consumed with dosa, ideal for cold climates! Serves 2 to 3 people.
Boil the toovar dal or cook it under pressure, then blend into a soupy consistency. Take a large pan, gently heat oil, add channa dal and urad dal until slightly red, stirring constantly. Add rai, hing; pop the rai seeds. Add the toovar dal and stir, add water, salt, haldi powder, garam masala, amli paste, and thoroughly stir. If thick, add more water. Leave to boil for at least 3/4 to an hour.
Dosa Dal (normal):
Make same as above, but use more boiled toovar dal (at least 2 to 2 1/2 cups). Add vegetables like diced aubergines and onions if desired. This thick and rich dal is delicious!
Dosa Stuffing (vegetable): Serves 2 to 3 people.
In a wok or karahi, heat oil over medium heat. When hot, add limbu leaves, urad dal, stir until slightly red. Add jeera seeds and rai seeds; pop the rai seeds. Add potatoes and onions, then salt and haldi powder. Cover and cook on medium to low heat until tender. Add remaining ingredients and stir. Cook until done. Optionally, add lemon juice.
Coconut Chutney:
In a small frying pan, gently heat oil. When hot, add urad dal until slightly red. Add rai and limbu; pop the rai seeds. Mix other ingredients in a bowl, then combine with the fried mixture. If tart, add sugar or substitute.
Dosa Pancake Method:
Once prepared the dal, chutney, and stuffing, heat a flat frying pan (or heavy-based griddle) over medium heat. Test if hot enough by adding a drop of pancake mixture; it should sizzle immediately. Pour about 1/2 cup mixture onto the pan, spreading into a circle with a ladle or moving the pan quickly. Add oil around the edges. Cook until done on one side, then gently lift and turn (first two always stick). Both sides cooked, place filling in the middle, fold over, and serve hot with dal or rassam and chutney/ketchup.
Granny's Tip:
Between pancakes, clean the pan with a kitchen towel soaked in water to help the next pancake cook evenly. Thinner pancakes yield better results. If possible, have someone else make them for you! Enjoy your dosas!
